title: Beginner Farm Table Bench
summary: The classic first build: a farmhouse dining bench from three 2x4s and half a 2x6, needing only a saw and a drill. About $20 in lumber, one afternoon, zero excuses.
description:
Every woodworker has a first project, and this bench is engineered to be a good one: two tools (a saw and a drill), four cut lengths, and a design where the only real skill is driving screws straight. The payoff is a genuinely useful 5-1/2-foot dining bench — the piece that seats three kids at a farm table.

The design hides a couple of teacher's tricks. The aprons sit 3/4" below the top of the legs, and the plan's method for getting that right is not measuring: rest the apron on a scrap of 1x or 3/4" plywood while you screw, and the offset sets itself. That's a jig, and realizing that scrap-as-spacer counts as a jig is the actual lesson of the build. The middle support does two jobs — it stops the long aprons from spreading, and it gives the seat boards a landing in the middle of their span.

The seat is the two halves of one 12-foot 2x6, side by side. Have the store cut it — a 12-footer doesn't fit in a car, and their saw is free. Building two benches? Buy five 2x4s instead of six; the cut list leaves enough spare.

tools.note: Or a circular saw with a speed square — the plan's whole premise is two tools.

materials.name: 2x6, 12 ft
materials.note: The seat — cut exactly in half at the store.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Legs, aprons, stretchers. Two benches? Five boards covers both.

materials.name: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", star drive

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two long aprons at 66", four legs at 16-1/2", four end aprons and stretchers at 7-1/2" and one middle support at 4-1/2". Cut two seat boards at 72" from the 2x6. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to two 12 ft 2x6. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the two leg sets
steps.body:
Screw each pair of legs to an end apron and a stretcher with 2-3/4" screws and glue. The trick for the 3/4" offset: lay a piece of 1x scrap or 3/4" plywood under the apron as a spacer while you drive — the apron ends up exactly 3/4" down from the leg tops without measuring anything.

Congratulations, you just made your first jig.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", star drive

steps.title: Attach the long aprons
steps.body: Screw the 66" aprons to the tops of the leg sets, into that 3/4" recess. The bench frame now stands on its own — set it on the flattest floor you have and check for rock before the glue grabs.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", star drive

steps.title: Add the middle support
steps.body: Find the center of the span and screw the little 4-1/2" block between the aprons. Small part, two jobs: it stops the aprons spreading apart over time, and it gives the seat boards something to be screwed to at mid-span so they never develop a bounce.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", star drive

steps.title: Screw down the seat
steps.body:
Center the two 2x6 seat boards — about 1" of overhang past the ends, 1/4" past the sides — and screw them to the legs, aprons, and middle support.

Ease the top edges with sandpaper (this seat meets the backs of knees), finish to match your table, and slide it under. First project, done, and someone will sit on it tonight.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, #9 x 2-3/4", star drive

images.alt: Simple farmhouse dining bench with 2x6 seat and 2x4 legs
